data.title: Regeneron: Q4 Earnings Preview - Why I'm Expecting A Beat And Strong 2026
data.description:
Investment Overview

This is a crucial time for the pharmaceutical industry, as following on from the flagship J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference in mid-January comes Q4 and full-year earnings reporting on January 30th.

Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (REGN), the Pharma based in Tarrytown, New York, will report earnings and business updates on January 30th and gave its presentation at JP Morgan on January 12th, so this feels like an ideal time to provide some updated coverage on the company.

In my last note on Regeneron for Seeking Alpha, reviewing Q3 earnings, I gave stock a Buy rating, which has proven the right call given shares have risen in value by >16% since publication, having already gained >15% immediately after earnings were announced.

Ultimately, however, Regeneron stock still trades some way below its all time high price of ~$1,200 per share, achieved in August 2024, stock having suffered a torrid bear run in the second half of 2024 and first half of 2025, sinking to <$500 per share by May last year, down nearly 60% in less that 12 months.

Regeneron Overview - Eylea The Past, Dupixent The Future?

In simplistic terms, the story of Regeneron and its success as a Pharma business can be told through two assets: the eye disease therapy Eylea (aflibercept), a vascular endothelial growth factor ("VEGF") inhibitor, and the immunology drug Dupixent (dupilumab), an interleukin-4 receptor alpha antagonist.

When Eylea was first approved to treat the "Wet" form of age-related macular edema, a leading cause of vision loss and blindness in Americans ages 60 and older, Regeneron stock traded ~$60 per share, and its success was largely responsible for Regeneron's growing revenues from $446m in 2011, to ~$6.6bn in 2019.

In that year, Eylea earned $4.64bn of revenues for Regeneron in the US, while its partner Bayer, the German Pharma, which markets and sells Eylea in Europe, paid Regeneron $1.14bn of collaboration revenues.

In 2022, those two figures were $6.3bn and $1.43bn, respectively, but since then revenues have been in decline - $4.8bn in 2024 and likely to be ~$2.6bn in 2025. This is due to the launch of numerous Eylea biosimilar/generic drugs and the launch of Vabysmo by the Swiss Pharma Roche (RHHBY), which has a less frequent dosing regime.

These declining revenues may have been chiefly responsible for Regeneron's recent share price declines, but it is not all doom and gloom. In 2023, Regeneron launched Eylea HD, a longer acting version of the original drug, dosed every 8-16 weeks, which is comparable to Vabysmo.

In 2024, Eylea HD earned $1.2bn in revenues, and in 2025, that figure looks set to rise to ~$1.64bn. During its presentation at JPM, Regeneron's long-term CEO and Chairman Leonard Schleifer told analysts:

Fourth quarter 2025 EYLEA HD net sales in the U.S. were $506 million, up 66% versus the fourth quarter of 2024 and up 18% sequentially versus third quarter of 2025 driven by a 10% increase in physician demand.

EYLEA's HD label was expanded in late November to include every 4-week dosing and the addition of macular edema following retinal vein occlusion, further enhancing its commercial potential. The mix of our franchise net sales has been steadily shifting towards EYLEA HD since launch with EYLEA HD now comprising nearly half of net sales.

Importantly, we submitted an application for a new filler for the EYLEA HD prefilled syringe with an FDA decision anticipated in the second quarter of 2026 this year, and the FDA also recently approved an alternate vial filler for EYLEA HD.

Nevertheless, the Eylea franchise, which earned ~$4.5bn in revenues in 2025, I'd estimate, is likely in terminal decline as the presence of generics in the market will only increase - I have the franchise earning ~$4bn in 2030, although that may be somewhat generous.

If Eylea is a foundational aspect of Regeneron's past, then its future in the short-to-medium is undoubtedly Dupixent, a drug first approved in the indication of Eczema in 2017, which has gone on to secure follow-on approvals in asthma, atopic dermatitis, nasal polyposis, Chronic Rhinosinusitis With Nasal Polyps, Eosinophilic Esophagitis, Prurigo Nodularis, Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease ("COPD"), Urticaria, and Bullous Pemphigoid.

The French Pharma Sanofi (SNY) markets and sells Dupixent, paying collaboration revenues to Regeneron. In 2024, Dupixent earned global revenues of $14.1bn, and Sanofi paid Regeneron collaboration revenues of $4.5bn, and in 2025, I'd estimate those two figures will be ~$17.7bn, and ~$5.86bn.

At the JPM conference, CEO Schleifer told analysts:

DUPIXENT has become the world's most widely used branded antibody with over 1.3 million patients actively treated worldwide. With annualized global net sales exceeding $19 billion based on third quarter net sales, which grew 27% year-over-year, DUPIXENT is positioned for sustained growth.

By 2030, I'd expect Dupixent to break the $20bn annual revenue barrier, and as I noted in my last post:

Furthermore, Regeneron is in the process of paying off a "development balance" which currently stands at ~$900m, related to its collaborations with Sanofi for Dupixent, Kevzara, and itepekimab - once paid off, no later than Q3 2026, Regeneron says, management expects a "significant increase in collaboration revenue."

What To Expect From Regeneron In 2026 & Beyond

Regeneron is not in the habit of providing revenue guidance, choosing to share R&D spend and SG&A spend forecasts instead, plus capital expenditures, tax rates, etc., but the Pharma easily surpassed analysts' expectations in Q3 (hence the share price gains), and analysts are expecting $3.76bn of revenues in Q4, normalized earnings per share ("EPS") of $10.63, and GAAP EPS of $8.99, according to Seeking Alpha.

I would expect Regeneron to beat on earnings when results are announced at the end of this month - my revenue figure would be ~$3.83bn - and would also expect the Pharma to beat on EPS also; my forecast would be for ~$11.6, based on a non-GAAP 9m figure of $34.83, as reported in the Q3 earnings release.

Two other important products for Regeneron are the checkpoint inhibitor Libtayo, with revenues of $1.2bn in 2024, which I would expect to earn close to $1.4bn in 2025, and Kevzara, indicated for rheumatoid arthritis - $460m revenues in 2024, and I'd expect ~$600m in 2025.

At JPM, CEO Schleifer told analysts:

LIBTAYO is now the only approved IO treatment for adjuvant CSCC an indication with 10,000 addressable patients in the U.S. alone, which we believe could be a blockbuster commercial opportunity in and of itself. LIBTAYO also continues to make significant inroads in lung cancer and is now the second most prescribed I-O treatment in first-line advanced lung cancer in the U.S.

It's notable that Libtayo won approval in an indication where Merck's (MRK) PD-1 inhibitor Keytruda, which will earn >$30bn of revenues in 2025 and is often considered the gold standard, has been unable to, and may bode well for the long-term growth of Libtayo.

I view Libtayo as being a potential $2.5bn selling asset by 2030. Kevzara could also achieve revenues >$1bn by 2030 if its current growth trajectory can be maintained. The cholesterol lowering drugs Evkeeva and Praluent will contribute >$400m in revenues in 2025 and >$500m in 2030, I'd speculate.

All things considered, I'd conclude that, internally, Regeneron would expect to break the $14.5bn revenue barrier in 2026, which would indicate an annual uplift of ~6%, which given the pipeline opportunities I will discuss below, ought to cheer Wall Street and help Regeneron shares - which are up >30% on a six-month basis - to continue to recapture their lost value.

Pipeline Opportunities

Regeneron's pipeline focuses on six therapeutic areas: ophthalmology, Immunology and Inflammation, Oncology, hematology, cardiovascular and metabolic, and neurology and rare disease, markets with a combined value of >$200bn, the Pharma estimates.

As we can see in the slide below (taken from its JP Morgan presentation), multiple new products may be brought to market in the next few years:

While the melanoma space is crowded with therapies competing to become the new standard-of-care, it is a multi-billion dollar market and Regeneron is confident its combo of Libtayo & fianlimab can outperform Keytruda in a pivotal study and secure approval with a best-in-class profile, while already approved Lynozyfic will take on Pharma's such as Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) in multiple myeloma markets, which are equally as large as melanoma.

Meanwhile cemdisiran and pozelimab (already approved to treat for Children and Adults with CHAPLE Disease as Veopoz) are complement inhibitors leveraging a short, interfering RNA approach, chasing approvals in indications such as generalised myasthenia gravis and Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria ("PNH"), areas of unmet need that support "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) revenue potential.

In hematology, the progress of two factor XI antibodies ought to be worth watching - as CEO Scliefer put it recently:

A lot of people have a mistaken belief that it's easy to make antibodies and all antibodies are created the same. I think what the data, if you look at it, as Len said, more fully human antibodies have been approved from our platform than any other platform, 10% to 20% of all approved antibodies come from our platform. And we repeatedly are producing the best agents with the least number of problems such as ADA, antidrug antibodies and so forth.

In summary, there are plenty of reasons for Regeneron's shareholders to look forward to 2026 and the next few years, in fact, with some optimism, although the most closely watched asset may well be olatorepatide, a GLP-1 receptor agonist - the same mechanism of action ("MoA") as Eli Lilly's (LLY) tirzepatide (Mounjaro / Zepbound) and Novo Nordisk's (NVO) semaglutide (Ozempic / Wegovy), which generated a combined >$60bn of revenues in 2025, I'd estimate.

The candidate was in-licensed from Chinese Pharma Hansoh and will be tested in combo with Praluent. Phase 3 results are due in China this year - if positive, I'd expect Regeneron's shares to respond with a double-digit percentage gain.

Almost as intriguing is the ATTR-CM indicated, in vivo gene therapy Nex-Z, being developed alongside Intellia (NTLA). Although this program has been undermined by safety setbacks, data arriving in H1 2026 could still lead to a Biologics License Application ("BLA") being submitted to the FDA, requesting commercial approval, and opening up an entirely new market that will be hard to ignore and could eventually produce blockbuster selling drugs.

Concluding Thoughts: I'm Optimistic Ahead Of Q4 Earnings, And In The Longer Term

I bought shares in Regeneron last year expecting them to swiftly recover from their initial crash from ~$1,200 to ~$750, but I have had to be patient, and a full recovery is still in the balance.

Nevertheless, my research indicates that Regeneron is in a good place at present, based on its products and pipeline. I have mapped out an optimistic scenario for the company between today and 2030 with multiple new product approvals, as shown in the table below:

The table suggests Regeneron could be a $23bn per annum revenue company by 2030. Naturally, not all the pipeline drugs will successfully make it to market, so I have been quite conservative with revenue figures, and it's also important to note that success of the GLP-1 drug, for example, would be far more significant for Regeneron's valuation and share prices than, e.g., a rare disease drug, given the massive market opportunity involved.

With Regeneron's profit margins typically being >30%, this makes the case for Regeneron stock being somewhat undervalued today (accepting caveats around pipeline success unexpected developments), and in fact, my discounted cash flow analysis suggests that, using a weighted average cost of capital of ~8.5%, Regeneron stock ought to be worth >$900 per share today.

I certainly think that is a reasonable target. 2025 was a year characterized by turmoil and change in the pharmaceutical landscape, with the Trump administration making sweeping changes to Departments such as the HHS, and FDA, and introducing radical new drug pricing policies. This being the second / third year of the administration, I'd expect less volatility, and I don't think it's unrealistic to forecast Regeneron stock to gain ~20% across the year.

Only time will tell, and of course, this prediction may not come to pass, but I remain optimistic, nonetheless, and also note the company reported >$8.4bn cash as of the end of Q3, long-term debt of ~$2bn - negligible, by Big Pharma standards - returned $3.8bn to shareholders via buybacks in 2025, and recently introduced a dividend - current yield, ~0.5%.

data.title: Clorox: Adding Sanitizers, But In Need Of Getting Its Hands Dirty
data.description:
As The Clorox Company (CLX) is starting 2026 with a substantial deal, it is time to update an investment thesis that goes back to the fall of 2023. At the time, I concluded that the business was struggling, with shares hitting lows driven by struggling operating performance and a prolific IT incident.

Still struggling today, Clorox has to get its hands dirty and improve the performance of its business, yet instead, it resorts to M&A and leverage, adding quite a bit of debt with the acquisition of hand sanitizer Purell.

About The Business

Clorox describes its business as a collection of leading and trusted brands. Some of these names include Pine-sol, Fresh Step, its namesake Clorox brand, Glad, ScoopAway, Poett, Kingsford, to name just a few.

These brands are sold in over a hundred countries, generating some $7 billion in sales; typically, these brands are number 1 or 2 in their respective categories. About a third of these sales are tied to health & wellness, nearly 30% from households, nearly 20% international, complemented by a smaller international business.

Despite these apparent great qualities, the reality is that Clorox has been struggling a bit in recent times, and with that, I mean years. Over the past decade, the company has only grown sales by about a billion, to around $7 billion, with that roughly 15% growth not really keeping track with inflation. This is also evident in the profitability, with dollar operating profits being flattish, indicating some margin contraction observed here. Of course, there was a temporary jump in the business performance that corresponded with the pandemic when people were cleaning more obsessively.

This could be offset by huge buybacks, but just a 5% reduction in the share count is rather modest over a period of a decade, creating a very tough road for investors to see real value creation, with earnings per share only advancing at a very modest pace. In Clorox's defense, dividends have been paid out, with a current yield far outpacing 4%, although much of the recent yield increase stems from recent share price declines.

All this is evident in the share price performance; a $100 stock in 2015 traded around the $150 mark pre-pandemic, to peak over $200 per share, but ever since it has been all downhill, with shares back to $110 per share, all being rather underwhelming.

Back To 2023

In the fall of 2023, the company has just reported a 4% increase in fiscal 2023 sales to $7.4 billion. GAAP earnings came in at apparently $1.20 per share, yet adjusted earnings were reported at $5.09 per share, with the discrepancy being impairment charges, as well as restructuring & improvement initiatives.

The four percent growth in sales looks better than it is, with all of this driven by pricing instead of volumes, which made me rather cautious, as a $130 stock with an adjusted power of $5 per share was not necessarily cheap, certainly not as Clorox furthermore operated with $2.2 billion in net debt, while EBITDA trended around a billion per annum.

Even a 2024 outlook, which called for adjusted earnings to advance to $5.60-$5.90 per share, did not alter my cautious stance, certainly not as a major IT breach meant that the full-year guidance could be thrown out of the window soon into the year.

Still Struggling

Fast forwarding to August 2024, the company reported a 4% fall in 2024 sales to $7.1 billion, driven by inflation slowing and some tiny asset sales announced in Argentina, but moreover a disappointing 6% decline in volumes.

However, cost reductions and cost control made the adjusted EBIT rose by 14% to $1.04 billion, driving a more than dollar per share improvement in earnings to $6.17 per share. GAAP earnings came in at $2.25 per share, with this discrepancy between both earnings metrics remaining considerable.

The business was set to shrink further, as the company announced the sale of the Better Health Vitamins, Minerals, and Supplements business, that business representing roughly 3% of sales. The earnings and growth picture remained very murky and complicated, not aided by the IT incident.

However, the company guided for 2025 adjusted earnings to advance to $6.55-$6.80 per share. Aided by easier comparables, offset by divestments, the company reported flat sales in the summer of 2025, with revenues reported dead flat at $7.1 billion. Adjusted earnings rose to $7.72 per share, this number now being much cleaner with GAAP earnings reported at $6.52 per share. The flattish sales were comprised of 5% organic sales growth, but that includes a pricing component as well, offset by divestments.

However, the issue is that of these trends not continuing in 2026, with the outlook being very soft. In fact, 2026 sales were seen down as much as 6-10%, with adjusted earnings seen down to $5.95-$6.30 per share. This is attributed to a built-up in shipments in 2025, thereby boosting the results that year, all done ahead of an ERP transition in the domestic business early in fiscal 2026.

And Now?

The anticipated softness in 2026 was clearly seen in the first quarter results for FY2026. First quarter sales were reported down 19% in November, with revenues reported at just $1.43 billion, causing adjusted earnings to be cut more than in half to $0.85 per share, this being related to the ERP transition in the quarter, also resulting in net debt ticking up to $2.5 billion here.

With 123 million shares trading around the $110 mark, this results in a $13.5 billion equity valuation and roughly $16 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at just over 2 times sales, with about 2 times leverage employed as earnings are difficult to see. Of course, adjusted earnings nearly approached $8 per share in 2025, but this includes artificial demand heading into a difficult fiscal 2026, in which earnings are seen around $6 per share. Working with a number in the middle, this results in a 15-16 times earnings multiple.

A Surprising Deal

In a period during which the business is focusing on the ERP transition and self-help to improve the overall performance, it is surprising to see Clorox announce a substantial acquisition.

In January, Clorox announced that it reached a $2.25 billion cash deal to acquire GOJO Industries, a leading skin health and hygiene solutions provider. Factoring in $330 million in net present value of tax synergies, the effective price drops to $1.92 billion, but it's fair to say that net debt will nearly jump to $4.7 billion here.

With this deal, the company will add Purell, a leading hand sanitizer brand, to the portfolio. The deal will add $800 million in sales to the business, thereby valued at 3 times sales (ahead of tax synergies), with revenues being quite stable, supported by 20 million soap and sanitization dispensers, which often need replenishments.

Factoring in tax synergies, the purchase price comes at 11.9 times EBITDA, suggesting a $161 million contribution on that front, for solid 20% margins, those largely in line with Clorox. The company targets $50 million in cost synergies over time, which is quite aggressive, equal to 6% of sales. So frankly, the deal multiples look fair, although Clorox will see net debt jump to $4.7 billion.

Pro forma EBITDA is seen around $1.3 billion based on the deal presentation, which increases the leverage ratio to 3.6 times. With the dividend running just a few pennies short of $5 per share, the payout ratio is high, and hence the potential to deleverage and perform buybacks is limited, although the tax payments could be pressured for a while. However, the company targets to reduce leverage to 2.5 times in 2027, which looks ambitious (absent of asset sales).

And Now?

In between shares have fallen another 15% over the past two years, stagnation has been the name of the game. This made the valuations gradually come in a bit.

However, the story remains very volatile, with no real structural growth seen. Furthermore, while the GOJO deal looks nice and adds quite a bit of leverage. While shares are definitely cheaper and more attractive, I fail to have conviction just yet.

data.title: First Citizens: Margin Pressure Comes Into Focus (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of First Citizens BancShares, Inc. (FCNCA) have been a poor performer over the past year, trading down 9%. Shares have been volatile, as investors feared potential private credit losses, given its Silicon Valley Bank unit, but thus far, credit metrics have been reasonable, allowing shares to recover.

On Friday, FCNCA reported mixed Q4 results and disappointing guidance, sending shares down nearly 10%. I last covered First Citizens in October, when I upgraded the stock to a Buy, and that recommendation has played out with FCNCA returning over 11% since then even with this pullback. With updated financials and shares surging past my $1,960 price target and now returning towards if, now is a good time to see if shares can perform better in 2026.

In the company’s fourth quarter, First Citizens earned $51.27, beating estimates by $7.48 as revenue grew by 1% to $2.4 billion. While First Citizens beat, this was a low-quality beat, driven by a reserve release that is unlikely to be sustained in 2026. Margins are under pressure, and this pressure is likely to persist, leaving FCNCA reliant on buybacks to avoid a year over year decline in EPS in 2026.

Balance sheet growth is strong

Looking first at its balance sheet, FCNCSA has $162 billion in deposits, down by $1.6 billion sequentially. This is primarily due to year-end seasonality, and deposits were up over $6 billion from last year. Deposit costs down a solid 37bps from last year, reflecting good pull-through from rate cuts. So far this cycle, FCNCA has passed on 52% of Fed rate cuts, not quite as much as the 64% of hikes it passed on during the hiking cycle, but still a good performance. Importantly, we have also seen growth in noninterest-bearing (“NIB”) balances, which appear to have bottomed. I view its deposit trends as unambiguously healthy.

On the other side of its balance sheet, loans grew by $3.2 billion to $148 billion. this was up over 5% from last year, a solid performance in a fairly challenging environment for loan growth. Loan yields fell by 20bps sequentially to 6.24%, given the cuts by the Fed. All of its Q4 loan growth came from its SVB unit, and loans now total $44 billion. Over the past year, $6.8 billion of its $7.7 billion of loans come from SVB. An improved funding environment for the technology and startup sector has helped to boost growth here.

While fundamentals and fundraising are currently strong, these firms are generally capital markets reliant to support their business, so the outsized growth contribution here does increase the overall sensitivity of FCNCA’s business to markets and VC fundraising.

But margin pressure intensifies

Unlike other regional banks, First Citizens does not have a substantial securities portfolio held at a loss. This has benefited its capital position, but as a result, it does not have the fixed reinvestment tailwind other banks are enjoying, leaving its net interest income (“NII”) more exposed to changes in the fed funds rate. Indeed, its net interest margin (“NIM”) compressed 4bps sequentially to 3.11%. NIM is down 5bps from last year. NII was still up by nearly 3%, thanks to ongoing balance sheet growth. The pace of NIM decline was disappointing.

This narrowing is the primary driver of weakness in shares in my view, as guidance points to further NIM compression in 2026. The compression in loan yields more than offset the benefit from lower deposit costs, and lower yields on its ~$20 billion of cash held at other banks as the Fed cuts rates further drive NIM down. While its better positioned securities portfolio has insulated the bank from unrealized losses that have hampered other banks, this means it does not have the positive fixed securities reinvestment tailwind to offset NIM pressures elsewhere across its balance sheet.

Reserves are low

Net charge-offs were 39bps, and during the quarter FCNCA released nearly $100 million of loan loss reserves, which drove the beat. Allowances now stand at $1.57 billion and cover 1.06% of loans, down from 1.2% of loans a year ago. Nonaccrual loans stand at $1.3 billion. While down sequentially, this was up over $100 million from last year. Allowances now just cover nonaccruals by 1.2x, which is extremely low. This is why investors are largely looking past the reserve-driven Q4 beat, as reserves are light.

I generally like to see reserves cover at least 1.5x of NPAs, implying reserves should be $400 million higher. Now, if the economy continues to grow and capital markets remain firm, this reserve level may prove appropriate, but FCNCA has limited room for error, and if we see an economic downturn, FCNCA will likely need to build reserves. Given the composition of the beat, this quarter was not as strong as it first appeared with NIM weak and reserves continuing to appear quite low.

First Citizens has a very strong capital position, which more than offsets its light credit reserves, though buybacks have eroded much of its capital buffer. Its tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio ended the year at 11.15%. I view 10.5-11% as an appropriate capital ratio for FCNCA, giving it excess capital for M&A and organic growth, as well as share repurchases.

FCNCA pays just a token dividend of 0.4%, focusing its capital returns instead on share buybacks. During Q4 it spent $900 million on buybacks. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 10% over the past year. With capital down 180bps over the past year, I expect the pace of buybacks to slow in 2026.

Guidance points to further NIM erosion; too soon to buy shares

Alongside results, management provided full year guidance. Interestingly while it expects deposits to grow by over $20 billion, loans will grow by less than $10 billon. The net interest guide of $6.7 billion is particularly disappointing as that would be down from $6.8 billion this year. This points to sharper NIM pressure, given the magnitude of its balance sheet growth. The relative lack of loan growth vs deposit growth will also be a headwind to the efficiency of its balance sheet. It expects net charge-offs to be similar to Q4 levels, and expense growth will remain elevated at ~4%.

Back in October, I saw FCNCA having $160-$170 of 12-month forward earnings power, alongside $100 of excess capital net of reserves. I expect net income to fall by $200-300 million in 2026 vs 2025, which will largely be offset by share repurchases. As such, I am targeting $162-$175 now, though excess capital net of reserves is closer to $50 after the elevated Q4 buyback.

Now over the past few months, we have seen earnings multiples expand across the regional banking sector, as credit fears ease and regulatory benefits become more apparent. Arguably, the 11x multiple I was previously using as a price target was overly conservative, though I struggle to see shares getting much past 12x, given the greater capital market sensitivity of SVB’s loan book, lower net income, and significant NIM pressure. At 11.5x plus its excess capital, I now see fair value or $1,980. I struggle to value FCNCA above this.

In other words, the 2026 outlook is not that different than I saw in October; however, shares got ahead of themselves, and guidance was a stark reminder that its NIM profile is worse than peers with FCNCA reliant on buybacks to keep EPS similar to the $178 reported this year. As such, I am downgrading shares to a Hold.

Even after this drop, FCNCA is fairly valued, and I would not rush to buy the dip. I would want a drop closer to $1850 before I would consider adding First Citizens.

data.title: 2 Factors Keeping Markets In Overbought Territory
data.description:
Markets have quickly recovered from their one-day sell-off Tuesday that saw the S&P 500 and NASDAQ fall over 2% on the day. However, tensions quickly de-escalated on Wednesday following a speech by the POTUS at Davos. Over the next two trading sessions, equities have recaptured almost all of their steep losses from Tuesday.

The buy-the-dip mentality is strong in this market, despite equities trading in very overbought territory viewed by myriad traditional valuation metrics. The Buffett Indicator, or the market-cap-to-GDP ratio, is in uncharted territory. This metric is far above its peak during the Internet Boom. This reading is also significantly above the levels in late 2021, just before investors saw the NASDAQ sell-off by a third in 2022.

Using the Shiller PE ratio or the S&P 500 dividend yield, the markets are trading near the peak of the Internet Boom levels.

So, what is helping to keep the market at these extreme valuation levels despite growing worries about a potential AI bubble forming?

I have written several times how the current period is quite different from 1999, which was the last time equities were trading at these valuations viewed via several traditional metrics. The demographics of the United States were more supportive from an economic perspective. The median age of the United States was more than four years younger than it is now. The labor participation rate was 500 bps higher, and the Baby Boomers were in their prime earning years.

Fiscally, the periods are like night and day. The United States ran a small fiscal surplus in 1999, and the federal debt was under $6 trillion. Currently, the U.S. federal debt is over $38 billion, and the country is running near $2 trillion in annual fiscal deficits despite the strong headline GDP growth.

Interest on the debt is now the second-largest line item on the federal budget, exceeded only by Social Security. The U.S. trade deficit in FY1999 was just $260 billion, as China had not joined the WTO yet. Clearly, from a fiscal and demographic perspective, the U.S. was in a much stronger position at the end of the 20th century than it is now.

However, this market has a couple of factors that help explain how these valuation levels are being maintained. The first is the rise of passive investing. Just over 60% of the trading volume of the market is driven by passive index investing. This type of investing has enjoyed a huge increase in recent years. The fact is the majority of new money coming into the market is on autopilot.

This is part of the reason for the huge divergence in performance between the megacaps in the market and the rest of the equities. For every dollar going into a passive S&P index fund, nearly 15 cents go into just two stocks: Alphabet (GOOG) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA). Both of which have larger market caps than the annual GDPs of either France or the U.K. Microsoft (MSFT) was the most valuable stock by market cap in 1999. It traded then at roughly one-third the annual GDP of France at the time as a way of comparison.

One of the consequences of this is significant multiple expansion throughout most of the Magnificent Seven. One of my favorite factoids involves Apple (AAPL). The tech giant's revenues are 70% higher than they were a decade ago. The stock's market cap has risen just under 700% over that time.

It is important to note that the Baby Boomer generational cohort owns nearly 55% of all stock market wealth. The oldest Baby Boomer turns 80 years old this year. More and more of this wealth will need to be liquidated to support living in retirement and to settle inheritances as this generation increasingly leaves this Earth. When/how this starts to impact passive investing flows is something to keep an eye on.

Another key factor behind why I think equities are continuing to trade at extreme valuations is many investors' belief in the Fed Put. Back in 1999, it was inconceivable to contemplate the massive governmental interventions that occurred during the Great Financial Crisis and during Covid. Given the massive increases to federal debt and expansion of the Fed's balance sheet, one has to wonder how much ammo is left for any further major interventions during the next crisis. At least without seriously debasing the U.S. dollar further. Something that explains the huge rise in gold prices in recent years.

My view continues to be that the stock market is in a bubble that will eventually pop. That said, while the music is still playing, one has to dance to some extent. This is why my portfolio has approximately three quarters of its allocation within covered call holdings on the few stocks I am finding in the market that still sport reasonable valuations. Almost all the rest is in cash and short-term treasuries awaiting much lower entry points when reality finally descends on the markets.

data.title: TSMC: Sell The Perfect Quarter, A Contrarian View (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (TSM) is an excellent company, but today, its stock is priced for perfect execution with sustained record profit margins, an elevated P/E multiple, and continued rapid growth due to the ever-rising AI investments. My bear thesis is that this setup is fragile.

If revenue growth declines to management's implied rate for 2027-29, profit margins revert to their historical means due to competition from Intel (INTC) and others, and the market participants rerate the stock simultaneously, the stock could drop 33 percent from current levels. Layering on top the non-trivial Taiwan Strait risk, I believe that TSMC's risk/reward profile looks tilted to the downside at current levels and rate the stock Sell.

Previous Coverage

Two years ago, I deemed TSMC a long-term winner due to its technological leadership, relationships with major players, and opportunities in AI and high-performance computing. Since then, the stock has surged 150 percent, rising nearly five times the S&P 500's 35 percent increase during the same period.

Blowout Results and Momentum

TSMC recently announced blowout Q4 2025 results, which included top-line and bottom-line beats, even better Q1 2026 guidance, exceptional margins driven by a favorable mix and strong demand for its leading-edge technologies, new investment announcements, multi-year growth signals, and a dividend hike. The results beat consensus expectations on all fronts, and as a result, TSMC stock surged as much as 7 percent the next day to new all-time highs.

Positioning and Sentiment Risk

The following shows that out of the 18 Wall Street analysts that follow TSMC, 11 analysts rate the company's stock a Strong Buy, 6 rate it Buy, and 1 rates it Hold. No Wall Street analyst currently rates the company Sell or Strong Sell.

The following shows that out of the 16 Seeking Alpha authors that published a TSMC article in the last 30 days, 6 rated the company's stock Strong Buy, 10 rated it Buy, and no author rated the stock Hold, Sell, or Strong Sell.

Even Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating for TSMC is Strong Buy with a very high score of 4.95. The following shows the Factor Grades systemically assigned to the stock, ranging from A+ for Profitability to C- for Valuation, as well as the Quant Rating History for the last three years:

When I see that every analyst and every author and even Seeking Alpha's quantitative model are all on the same side of a trade, I see a warning sign.

Fundamental Analysis

The following graph illustrates that TSMC's trailing-twelve-month gross profit, operating profit, and net profit margins are currently at all-time record highs:

The above graph also shows, however, that TSMC's high profit margins are a recent phenomenon and that, B.C. ChatGPT, the company's profit margins generally trended in a relatively stable range and about one-quarter to one-third lower than where they are today. For example, the above graph shows that TSMC's operating profit margin on a trailing twelve-month basis oscillated a few percentage points around 35 percent for 15 years from 2005 to 2020, before rising in recent years to 51 percent as of the most recent period.

Bear Case: Mean Reversion

In the coming years, there is a material probability that TSMC's profit margins will drop back to their previous ranges, especially if Intel (INTC) is successful in its turnaround efforts. In that case, TSMC's margins could materially drop.

Furthermore, TSMC's price-to-earnings ratio is also above its historical range: Before 2022, TSMC's P/E ratio ranged between 9x to 28x, and a reversion to the mean of 16x, especially if combined with a reversion to the mean of the company's profit margins, would lead to a material drop in stock price.

TSMC's profit margins are not the only part of its income statement at risk of peaking; its revenue growth rate is also likely to decline in the coming years.

Bear with me.

Last week, TSMC's management materially increased its long-term growth outlook:

We now expect our overall long-term revenue growth to approach a 25% CAGR, in US dollar terms, for the five-year period starting from 2024.

Considering that revenue grew 36 percent in 2025, and assuming that TSMC achieves its revenue growth target of 30% in 2026, the management's above long-term revenue growth forecast implies a 20% CAGR for 2027-2029, or a material decline in the company's growth rate to its long-term average.

Furthermore, the following graph illustrates that the analyst consensus for the company's long-term EPS CAGR has risen to 30 percent for the coming years:

Assuming that revenue grows at a 20 percent CAGR from 2027 to 2029, TSMC's profit margins would need to increase materially from their already record high levels for EPS to grow at a 30 percent CAGR. Readers should note that TSMC historically has limited buybacks to offsetting dilution from employee stock-based compensation, as the following graphs illustrate:

Geopolitical Risk

TSMC's significant geopolitical risk lies in the fact that its critical manufacturing footprint is concentrated in Taiwan, making the company exposed to escalation in the Taiwan Strait in one or more forms of coercion or shipping/insurance disruption or blockade or even direct conflict in the coming years.

I believe that the likelihood of this risk materializing is higher than what TSMC investors believe given this comment by President Xi Jinping:

The historical task of the complete reunification of the motherland must be fulfilled, and will definitely be fulfilled.

Valuation

In valuing the company, I make the following assumptions:

  1. Continued decline in growth rate;
  2. Mean reversion in profit margins;
  3. Mean reversion in the P/E multiple;
  4. Stable number of shares outstanding.

I forecast that the company's revenue will grow from $121 billion in 2025 to $150 billion in 2026 and $180 billion in 2027, while its net profit margin declines to 35 percent in 2027, or $63 billion in net GAAP profit in 2027.

I apply a P/E multiple of 16x, in line with the company's historical profitability levels, and calculate an intrinsic equity value of $1 trillion, or $222 per share, representing a 33% drop from the current stock price of $333 per share.

Bullish Risks to My Bear Thesis

The company's stock price has put up incredible momentum in recent months, rising nearly 50 percent from $228 per share on September 2. If geopolitical risks do not materialize in the coming months, this strong momentum could push the stock higher in the near term.

Further, if Intel and other competitors cannot execute on their ambitions, then TSMC could continue to charge its customers relatively high profit margins, which would invalidate my "mean reversion in profit margins" assumption.

Bottom Line

TSMC just delivered a blowout quarter and raised long-term expectations, but that is precisely why the stock is currently priced for perfect execution. I believe that sentiment around the stock has extended too far into optimism and that a mean reversion could lead to a material drop in the stock price. I assign a $222 per share price target for 2026 and downgrade the stock to Sell.

data.title: Bausch Health: RED-C Failure Leaves Blockbuster Xifaxan Without A Successor
data.description:
Bausch Health Companies Inc. (BHC) stock is off nearly 10% in intraday trading after reporting that its Phase 3 program evaluating "next-gen" rifaximin to delay the first episode of hepatic encephalopathy in patients with cirrhosis failed.

The article that follows takes a closer look and focuses on the implications of this recent development.

Rifaximin

As everyone is already aware, Bausch is busy deleveraging. As of September 30, 2025, Bausch’s long-term debt was approximately $21 billion.

XIFAXAN (rifaximin) is Bausch’s blockbuster drug that is FDA-approved to reduce the risk of overt hepatic encephalopathy recurrence in adults as secondary prevention. I feel like every time I am on the floor as a nurse, I am administering it. XIFAXAN is the primary contributor (85% in FY24) in the company’s Salix segment, which generated $716 million in revenue in Q3 2025.

Because XIFAXAN’s exclusivity is nearing an end within a couple of years (~2028), the company was positioning the Phase 3 RED-C program of “amorphous rifaximin solid soluble dispersion” as a new rifaximin approach. It was specifically designed to enhance the GI luminal solubility of legacy rifaximin. Of course, the company's main hope is to extend the life cycle of the blockbuster franchise. For context, XIFAXAN made up ~20% of FY24 revenue, generating nearly $2 billion. RED-C would’ve significantly increased rifaximin’s total addressable population as well.

One of the Phase 3 study registries (NCT05297448) for the RED-C program states that the primary outcome was “time to first events of overt hepatic encephalopathy requiring medical intervention or death,” with an outcome timeframe of 72 weeks. In the announcement, the company stated that it is reviewing the full data set to determine potential new development opportunities and did not publish numerical efficacy results other than stating that the primary endpoints were not met.

The setback especially hurts because XIFAXAN is likely a higher-margin product for Bausch. So, its performance is critical to the company’s ability to generate cash and deleverage.

Financials

Briefly covering Q3 2025 results, consolidated revenue was up 7% year-over-year to $2.68 billion. XIFAXAN was cited as the key contributor, growing 16% year-over-year. The company ended the third quarter with $1.3 billion in cash and cash equivalents. Cash provided by operating activities was $405 million in Q3. The company's net interest expense was $397 million in Q3.

With interest expenses as high as they are, the company is financially constrained. A lot of operational gains are consumed by debt service. Moreover, while its average cost of debt was roughly 8% (a pre-tax weighted average) per recent financial statements, the December 2025 exchange of $1.6 billion in notes into a 10% coupon due 2032 is a cost of “buying time.”

As for its 2025 outlook, the company expects to generate roughly $1 billion in adjusted cash flow from operations.

Now What?

With RED-C now negative, Bausch’s drug pipeline becomes pretty dependent on larsucosterol developments. The company added this asset after acquiring DURECT for just $63 million upfront in mid-2025. The asset is nearing a registrational Phase 3 program for severe alcohol-associated hepatitis.

Larsucosterol is described as an “endogenous sulfated oxysterol and a small molecule epigenetic regulator.” The asset has the FDA's Breakthrough Therapy designation, and there are currently no approved therapies for severe alcohol-associated hepatitis. This is a pretty serious condition that is cited as having a 30% mortality rate within 90 days of hospitalization.

In the Phase 2a open-label dose-escalation trial, 19 patients received one or two intravenous infusions. The publication reports that all 19 treated patients survived the 28-day study.

In the Phase 2b trial, patients were randomized 1:1:1 to receive low-dose, high-dose, or placebo. The primary outcome was 90-day mortality or liver transplant. Although both drug arms saw numerical reductions in 90-day mortality (25/103 for placebo versus 15/102 and 17/102 for drug), it did not reach statistical significance versus placebo.

So this will be a challenging indication to win in Phase 3 (planned, with data expected in 2028), and, even if successful, does not appear to be anywhere near as lucrative a market as hepatic encephalopathy.

Valuation Assumptions

Because Bausch is so debt-dependent, modeling it is a bit different from what I'm used to. So the following is my best attempt.

Year 1 revenue of approximately $10.5 billion is the consensus analyst estimate for 2026. Revenue growth and free cash flow margin of 4% and 10%, respectively, are my estimates and are grounded in recent financial performance.

Most everything else is either standard DCF model methodology or was derived from the company's most recent financial statements.

As usual, stock valuations are made up primarily of “terminal values.” This is the sum of discounted cash flows after the initial nine-year projection. While the company is not going to feel an immediate impact on its bottom line from RED-C failure, it will have a meaningful impact on long-term growth.

Final Words

While I see the ubiquity of XIFAXAN every shift on the hospital floor as a nurse, as an analyst, I see a blockbuster nearing its end without a clear successor. And I am afraid that larsucosterol is merely a high-risk asset in a challenging acute clinical space that has yet to prove its merit. Meanwhile, the company remains on a financial “treadmill.”

So, while BHC stock may appear undervalued with P/E values in the low single digits, recent developments remind us that a cheap valuation is often just a reflection of a shortening runway. Until the company can show a clear path to deleveraging that doesn't involve trading 8% debt for 10% debt, investors should view BHC stock with caution (Hold rating).

data.title: McCormick: 'Buy' The Dip Opportunity Is Here (Upgrade)
data.description:
Earnings season can be a great time to pick up bargains, as knee-jerk reactions can result in material price swings. This creates opportunities for investors with an investment time horizon beyond 1–2 years and can wait out near term volatility. That’s what I call time arbitrage, and is one way that retail investors can beat professional money managers who are only focused on the near term.

This brings me to McCormick & Company, Incorporated (MKC), which I last covered a while back in May 2021. At that time, it and most consumer staples stocks were highly favored by the market due to pandemic era pantry stocking. I recommended sitting on the sidelines at the time due primarily to valuation concerns.

That appears to have been a prescient call as the stock has since fallen by 32% (-24.5% total return including dividends), underperforming the S&P 500 (SPY). This was driven by slowing topline growth (from the ~20% growth it saw during 2021) and margin pressures from cost inflation. At the current price of $61.20, MKC sits close to its 52-week low after its recent earnings report, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit MKC including recent business results, and discuss why now may finally be an opportune time to "Buy" the stock while market sentiment is working against it, so let’s dig in!

Why MKC?

McCormick & Company is a global leader in flavors with a broad suite of spices, seasoning mixes, and condiments. It serves retail consumers, food manufacturers and foodservice businesses in more than 150 countries and territories. MKC’s iconic brands include its namesake spices, French’s, Frank’s RedHot, Cholula hot sauce, and Lawry’s, to name a few.

MKC’s diversified footprint means that it benefits from both consumers eating away from home and at home. This makes MKC more or less recession resilient due to its steady revenue base. MKC’s scale also enables it to generate above-average margins. As shown below, it scores a B- for Profitability with strong EBITDA and Net Income margins of 19% and 11.5%, respectively. MKC also has a strong return on equity of 14%.

MKC saw modest yet resilient results during Q4 2025. This was in light of cost inflation due to elevated commodity costs and tariffs. MKC’s organic sales rose by 2% YoY, driven by consumer organic sales growth of 3% over the prior year period. This segment also saw sustained volume gains for the seventh consecutive quarter, which is a notable achievement amidst challenged consumer spending.

However, higher commodity costs and tariffs pressured MKC’s bottom line. Gross margin contracted by 120 basis points during Q4 due to the aforementioned factors. These effects were partially offset by SG&A efficiencies and continuous improvement initiatives.

Nonetheless, MKC saw volume growth across major geographies of the Americas, EMEA, and Asia Pacific. Sales for the Consumer segment were led by EMEA, which posted 8.5% sales growth. This was followed by 3.1% growth in the Americas and 0.7% growth in APAC, as shown below.

Looking ahead, MKC remains well positioned as the global leader with premium brands. This is reflected by sustained or improving market share across key categories for six straight quarters. MKC is also expanding its product portfolio through product innovation and renovation. This includes the refreshed McCormick Gourmet collection, which has resonated well with consumers.

Moreover, MKC continues to gain distribution in high-growth channels like e-commerce. Management also sees significant opportunities to grow its presence on social media. MKC could also see higher growth in its Flavor Solution and QSR (quick service restaurants) segments. This is supported by momentum in reformulation projects and strong QSR volumes, particularly in the Americas, China, and Southeast Asia.

Importantly, MKC stands to benefit from the increasingly health-conscious consumers who are shying away from high-carb and high sugar foods. At the same time, the Consumer business represents around 60% of MKC’s sales and could benefit from increased cooking at home, as noted during the recent earnings call:

Health and wellness trends continue to gain momentum. Consumers are preparing healthier, more affordable meals at home, while exploring new flavors and culinary creativity. High protein and better-for-you claims are driving purchase trends across retail and foodservice. In addition, convenience paired with flavor exploration remains an area where consumers are willing to pay more.

In addition, consumers continue to stretch meals across multiple occasions and seek affordable ways to prepare fresh home-cooked meals. The consumer continues to show resilience by increasing their demand for value and behaviors that enable them to stretch their budget. These behaviors reinforce the importance of flavor in everyday cooking with herbs and spices continuing to lead center store unit consumption.

MKC maintains a solid balance sheet with a BBB credit rating from S&P. This is supported by a safe net debt to TTM EBITDA ratio of 2.94x. This supports the 3.1% dividend yield, which is well-covered by a 60% payout ratio. MKC is a dividend aristocrat with 39 consecutive years of raises. It also signaled confidence in its underlying earnings growth with a 7% dividend increase last month, which sits in line with its 5-year dividend CAGR of 7.6%.

Lastly, I see value in MKC after the recent drop in price to $61.20 with a forward P/E of 20.3, sitting below its historical P/E of 23.5. At the current valuation, the market appears to be wary of MKC’s low single digit topline growth and recent margin pressures.

However, the market may be ignoring drivers such as shifting consumer behavior toward healthier eat-at-home options and management’s guidance for margin expansion this year. MKC also has opportunities to counter margin pressures from strategic price increases and extracting inefficiencies, as it had done in 2025.

Analysts estimate around 7% annual EPS growth over the next 2 years. This, combined with the 3.1% dividend yield, MKC’s economy of scale, and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation could drive double-digit total returns in the low to mid-teens in the medium term.

Risks to MKC include input cost volatility due to crop yields, weather events, and geopolitical events. Tariffs could also be a headwind as that could increase costs of raw materials. Consumer pullback on spending on premium brands could impact MKC as well. Although it also manufactures for private label brands, these come with lower price points and margins.

Investor Takeaway

McCormick & Company has entered a more attractive risk-reward range after the recent price drop. The stock now trades near its 52-week low and sits at a discount to its historical valuation. It has resilient volume trends, steady market share gains, and clear long-term tailwinds from health-conscious, at-home cooking behavior.

Near-term headwinds from commodity costs and tariffs remain. However, these look manageable given management’s efficiency initiatives and guidance for margin improvement. Lastly, MKC offers a solid value setup with a well-covered 3.1% dividend and ~7% annual EPS growth potential. As such, given MKC’s moat-worthy brands and scale, I rate MKC as a "Buy" after the recent drop.

data.title: Hercules Capital: Last Man Standing As Internal BDCs Move To Monthly Dividends
data.description:
Hercules Capital (HTGC) is paying out a well-covered double-digit dividend yield that could be moved to a monthly payment schedule in line with its peers. While there hasn't yet been official guidance from the business development company ("BDC") that it intends to move to a monthly distribution schedule, the direction of the wind is clear. To be clear, there are dozens of BDCs, but only five are internally managed. Main Street Capital (MAIN), Capital Southwest (CSWC), Trinity Capital (TRIN), SuRo Capital (SSSS), and HTGC. SSSS is an equity-focused BDC with lumpy dividends that reflect exits on its venture-capital portfolio. Hence, there is really only four internally managed BDCs for income investors, and most have switched to monthly dividend distributions.

HTGC's most direct venture-debt focused peer, TRIN, was the most recent BDC to switch to monthly dividends. This essentially leaves HTGC as the last man standing, a position that could very well see the BDC make the switch to a monthly distribution this year. HTGC last declared a quarterly base cash dividend of $0.40 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.60 per share annualized for an 8.5% dividend yield. There was also a $0.07 per share supplemental dividend, which means a roughly 1.5% incremental yield when annualized. HTGC has been consistent with paying out an aggreagte $0.47 per share quarterly distribution, with the current 10% dividend yield providing for a 573 basis points spread to the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y). The CME FedWatch Tool currently has 50 basis points of rate cuts through 2026 as its base case for the year at a 32.1% probability.

Common Share Dividend Coverage And The Baby Bonds

I last rated HTGC as a buy, with this sentiment unchanged on the back of a possibly slower pace of Fed rate cuts in 2026, coverage of the double-digit yield, and continued robust access to institutional capital. The BDC realized fiscal 2025 third quarter net investment income ("NII") of $88.6 million, or $0.49 per share, to provide 104% coverage of the aggregate dividend payment. HTGC's coverage of the base dividends is significantly larger at 122%. The BDC's total investments at fair value were $4.31 billion, up 17.8% from $3.66 billion at the start of the year. This growth reflects positive cadence with originations, with the third quarter seeing originations of around $846 million to drive record originations of $2.87 billion for the first 3 quarters of 2025. HTGC is well on track to outpace its 2024 total of $3.12 billion.

Total investment income came in at a new record at $138.1 million, growth of roughly 10.3% year-over-year as assets under management ("AUM") closed out the third quarter at $5.5 billion. AUM was also up 20.7% over its year-ago comp. Critically, HTGC is notching records with its core metrics to form a moat against what's set to be a more Darwinistic backdrop for BDCs from a declining Fed funds rate environment. The BDC's investment portfolio is highly sensitive as it's built with 97.8% floating rate loans, albeit with interest rate floors. As there were another 50 basis points of rate cuts in the fourth quarter, HTGC should expect to see an incremental $0.05 per share hit to earnings. However, the dampening effects of the rate cuts would only be fully reflected in the BDC's fiscal 2026 first quarter.

Assuming the BDC's portfolio remained static, coverage of the base and aggregate dividend could drop significantly. This means HTGC's public trading 6.25% Notes due 10/30/2033 (HCXY) form the only other public security for prospective investors looking to build a position in HTGC, but without the risk of slipping dividend coverage. These pay out a $1.56 annual coupon per note for a 6.14% yield on cost as they're currently trading hands for $25.39 per share ahead of their 30th of October 2033 maturity. The commons still, in my opinion, provide a better risk and reward profile with the income substantial enough and current coverage set to be bolstered by ongoing record originations.

Conclusion

HTGC had 2 of its portfolio companies on nonaccrual at the end of the third quarter with a cost of $52.2 million, around 1.2% of its total investment portfolio at cost and far lower than the BDC average of 2.3%. If the BDC can keep its nonaccruals below the industry average at cost, continue to ramp up originations in 2026, and transition to a monthly dividend, the ticker should capture a large share of a possible liquidity flight from BDCs that have not capably scaled their portfolio to handle the current macroeconomic backdrop of falling rates. I am rating HTGC as a buy.

data.title: PENN Entertainment: The Market Gave Up, But Maybe Too Early
data.description:
I like the gaming industry, especially when it's more closely linked to real estate. I have a position in VICI Properties (VICI), I'm bullish on Red Rock Resorts (RRR), and more recently, I decided to look at PENN Entertainment (PENN), which, despite not being as solid as VICI, as it's not a REIT but rather an operator, is also a case that intrigued me greatly. But it's worth mentioning that this is a very different case from the others. At first glance, it seems to be a terrible case, as there's some “confusion” related to the digital strategy, a large loss, and the valuation seems to indicate a value trap, with a P/B below 1x.

But still, looking deeper into the case, I don't think it's as bad as the market seems to suggest. We're still talking about a company with several casinos around the US that manages to deliver minimally interesting results. In addition, management is trying to adjust strategies to remain relevant in both the physical and digital worlds.

Therefore, despite the relevant risks, I consider PENN stock a cautious buy.

PENN Entertainment: Starting with the Bad News

Before talking about the potential that PENN may eventually unlock, it's necessary to understand why this stock seems so cheap. A few quarters ago, the main strategy seemed to be digital expansion, with iGaming and sports betting, and one of the ways to achieve this was through an agreement with Disney's (DIS) ESPN.

More recently, PENN withdrew from the agreement with ESPN, explaining that it was difficult to scale ESPN bet:

“Although we made significant progress in improving our product offering and building a cohesive ecosystem with ESPN, we were unable to establish ESPN BET as a scale player. It is the right time to realign our interactive focus, prioritizing our digital assets in Canada and our Hollywood iCasino product to further leverage our core retail casino business and overall omnichannel business model. This shift emphasizes cross-sell opportunities across our ecosystem and enhances connectivity with our 33 million-plus PENN Play customer database.”

In part, this is also related to the competitive environment. Try to think of some form/alternative of betting on sports. There are several. There's DraftKings (DKNG) and other large listed companies, and we can also think of alternatives such as Polymarket.

This, in itself, was something that the market penalized, and rightly so. The company has casinos and hotels, but the main focus was on the digital scale, which is a growing market but extremely competitive. But beyond that, the execution and confusion of this strategy were also penalized. The partnership with ESPN not only went wrong, but they also failed to deliver anywhere near what they were aiming for, which was a much more substantial market share.

In other words, it's as if there is now a lack of confidence in PENN Entertainment's execution. There's still an important physical infrastructure, but it's practically ignored by the market since one of the main focuses of management is still digital, and recently the execution has not brought good results.

But that's only part of the uncertainty because if you treat digital as an optionality, if it works, great; if not, it doesn't matter to the core business. However, this is also reflected in the numbers. For example, the net loss for Q3 was $865 million, and most of that was impairment losses, which in that quarter amounted to $825 million. And most of that impairment is related to the “interactive” segment.

This reflects that the company invested heavily in this and now recognizes that the investments were not so well made. Another reflection of the digital “disaster” is that, in addition to low revenue, adjusted EBITDA was negative at $76 million, and the company itself called this “below expectations” in its release.

All of this worsens the perception of the company's capital structure, which has a net debt of ~$2.1 billion, while operating income, excluding impairment and depreciation, is only ~$160 million per quarter, i.e., it's a leverage of more than 3x even after adjusting this operating income.

Why I Still See Value in PENN Entertainment

But even in this very challenging context, to say the least, I still see some value in PENN. The first point is that most of the revenue, and especially EBITDA, comes from its physical structure. Therefore, this is the core of the case, and it's the main part that should weigh in an assessment.

The interactive segment was poor, as mentioned, but in Q3, “Retail Property” revenue was $1.4 billion, with a slight improvement YoY, with the company mentioning that “demand was generally stable” in the core. Adjusted EBITDAR for this segment was also $465 million, with margins of 32%, meaning it's still a very profitable segment that can be called a cash cow.

Another point, already mentioned, is that the result looks very bad, but this is due to non-recurring items. YoY, we can see an increase in consolidated revenue, and the only item that was really “worrying” in expenses was impairment losses, which is not a recurring item.

If we remove these impairment losses and depreciation, we already have an operating income of $160 million for the quarter, or a capacity to generate something close to $640 million per year. And this is very much in line with the valuation. We're talking about a company that currently has only $1.8 billion in market cap, and even if we add what the company calls “traditional debt,” we still have an EV of $4 billion, which is an EV-to-OI of 6x. If you look at it from an accounting perspective, this EV would be much higher, since the “correct” thing to do would be to incorporate leases into this debt, which makes it much more inflated and causes it to exceed $10 billion, and even using this metric, it's still possible to find an interesting valuation for the case.

Another interesting metric is the P/B. I don't think it's such an important metric to anchor, but currently the P/B is below 1x, as you can see in the table below. It still seems to hold some downside, but at the same time, it shows the market's disbelief, which is basically saying that PENN is worth the same as what it has on its balance sheet.

Note that so far I haven't mentioned much about digital, because for me, it's not such a central pillar. But still, the company needs to at least improve this segment so that the core can move forward. And with this prospect, I can be minimally optimistic. In the image below, PENN shows that retail customers are increasing penetration and contributing to digital growth. So, thinking about it this way, digital becomes something more complementary to the core, a way to monetize the same customer more.

In addition, the company is managing to significantly grow its digital database, with more than 5 million customers. It seems quite feasible to me that in a second phase, with a certain amount of operational leverage, the company will at least be able to stabilize operations and extract the minimum profitability from this business, which, together with a healthy core, would be enough to unlock a rerating for PENN stock.

The Bottom Line: Not Easy, But Asymmetrical

This subtitle sums up what I think of the PENN case. I don't think it's an easy case. In fact, I think management still has a lot to prove in terms of execution, whether they will be able to stabilize in the digital space, and whether the core can maintain minimally healthy growth. But I also think that the valuation today is asymmetrical. The downside seems low, supported by a compressed P/B ratio and low multiples, and if execution is at least reasonable, it is already possible to glimpse some upside.

Therefore, my rating for PENN is a cautious buy.

data.title: Silver: What's Different This Time?
data.description:
Silver has historically traded as an extremely volatile commodity, plagued with a high level of speculation. Accordingly, market tops have traditionally been followed by violent downside moves into bear market territory, as illustrated in the chart below.

Currently silver is trading at an all-time high near $95, deep into overbought territory. The looming question is whether the current price of silver is topping, soon to be followed by a retreat as shown by the chart in 1980 and 2011.

In 2026, however, there are multiple factors that should portend a much different outcome than in past bull markets, discussed below.

Silver shortage

Silver has experienced a 5-year physical deficit with mine production well under demand, documented by The Silver Institute World Silver Survey. During the last five years, demand has exceeded supply by 100 to 200 million ounces. It is noteworthy that mine production in 2011 was significantly higher in 2011 at 1160 million ounces compared to 2024 production of 819.7 million ounces reported in the World Silver Survey. From the above numbers, it is apparent that there has been underinvestment in mining, which could limit silver production for a decade or more.

A major problem with supply is due to the fact that it takes about 5-10 years to explore, develop, and permit a new silver mine. In addition, there are only a handful of mines that are primary silver producers. Most silver is produced as a byproduct of mining other minerals like gold, copper, zinc, and lead. Consequently, about 70% of silver is mined as a byproduct that is limited by the mining of other minerals.

In the interim, only increased recycling can add to supply significantly. However, there is only a limited amount of above-ground silver available due to widespread use in electronic devices that are not recycled.

Rough estimates of above-ground silver range between about 6-15 billion ounces in everything from electronic devices to silverware and jewelry. It's likely that recycling will increase markedly with increases in silver prices, but a looming question remains on how much silver can be recycled. For example, there are only a limited number of refineries and smelters in the U.S. positioned for recycling.

China export restrictions

Beyond the foregoing, a further reduction in silver supply will occur due to China placing restrictions on silver exports, requiring licensing to export the metal. China's action will likely have a big impact on world silver supply since it is the second largest producer of silver behind Mexico, documented by the World Silver Survey linked above.

In addition, silver is trading about $10 higher on the Shanghai futures exchange compared to the COMEX/US (shown on the line chart below), indicating extraordinarily high demand for silver in China.

Increasing demand

Increasing demand for silver is being fueled by exponential growth in electronics. Silver is the most electronically conductive commodity available and, accordingly, is being demanded widely in the ongoing growth of electrification of the world.

As noted in The World Silver Survey, solar panels (photovoltaics) alone are consuming nearly 200 million ounces annually. Beyond solar panels, electrification is growing exponentially in consumer electronics, electric vehicles, data centers, and countless other applications.

Recently it was disclosed that Samsung has developed a prototype solid-state battery pack for electric vehicles that will enable a driving range of about 600 miles with a charging time of about nine minutes. The battery pack will require up to a kilogram of silver (35.274 ounces) per battery pack. Simple arithmetic can illustrate the increase in demand for just a few thousand EVs deploying the Samsung battery pack.

World market for silver

The current world market for silver is far different from in historical bull markets. While most historical bull markets occurred primarily in the U.S., currently worldwide markets are increasing demand exponentially, primarily in China and India. Both countries are rapidly deploying solar panels to build electrical infrastructure to provide cleaner energy extending to remote areas where little or no electricity previously existed. Further, China is an industrial power far beyond what existed historically, while India continues to build its industrial base.

U.S. critical mineral designation

In addition to the foregoing, the United States designated silver as a critical mineral in November 2025. Accordingly, the U.S. will likely begin stockpiling silver, exerting additional pressure on available supply. It should be pointed out that a significant amount of silver is used in military hardware, especially advanced military hardware like missile systems. Interestingly, military demand is not included in the World Survey.

Currency debasement

As the U.S. continues to print money, many investors hedge their portfolios with hard assets that do not debase, like silver. The chart below illustrates growth in the M2 money supply, which has been accelerating in recent years. Silver, as both a precious and monetary metal, provides a more than ample hedge to currency debasement.

Investment demand

Investment demand for silver is increasing, which is apparent in the price movement to all time highs. It is also apparent that few generalist investors have precious metals in their portfolios. Accordingly, some investment advisors have begun to recommend a portion of their client's portfolios include commodities, including predious metals like gold and silver, documented in a Goldman Sachs report. Since this trend has only recently begun, it is likely there much more upside to investors moving into the precious metals space.

Geopolitical risks

Recent years have witnessed a rapid increase in geopolitical turbulence with wars, tariff wars, and other world-wide turbulance. Elevated geopolitical risks will likely continue for several years, unpinning demand for safe-haven assets like gold and silver.

Investing in silver

There are two primary ways to invest in silver--investing in ETFs tied to the pride of silver and investing in silver mining stocks. Two key ETFs that track the price of silver are the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) and the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV).

There are two key silver miners ETFs, Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL), and Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (SILJ). While the junior miners carry more risk, they also have more upside potential due somewhat to buyout prospects.

Investing in individual mining stocks should be advoided by investors unfamiliar with the mining industry. Without a thorough knowledge of how to analyze mining company prospects, it should be left to professionals. A more thorough discussion of the foregoing is provided in a previous SA article.

Risks

Silver has been an extremely volatile commodity with wide price movement both to the upside and downside. Investors should anticipate wide price swings both before and after investing in the silver market. Investing in silver is not for the "faint of heart."

Likely outcome for silver and final thoughts

With silver trading near all-time highs, $95 at this writing, there is reason to speculate that silver is long overdue for a significant correction as it approaches the $100 mark. Once silver breaks through the $100 mark, there is likely to be significant selling with a $100 selling target. While a correction can come at any time, it is likely to be short-lived due to unprecedented demand for physical silver. While futures markets previously determined the price of silver, physical demand has taken command of the market.

News is spreading of tight silver supplies and it is likely some industrial players are taking notice with purchases to ensure their supply. Without silver, many industrial products just cannot be manufactured. Indeed Samsung has contracted with Silver Storm Mining, locking in a 2-year supply. Others are likely to follow.
